qa_pairs.question: Once you have a RAPIDS Apptainer image, what can you do with it on a cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Once a RAPIDS Apptainer image is ready in your account, you can request an interactive session on a GPU node or submit a batch job to Slurm if your RAPIDS code is prepared.

qa_pairs.question: Which type of Docker image should a RAPIDS Apptainer image be based on to allow interactive work with Jupyter Notebooks on a GPU node?
qa_pairs.answer: An Apptainer image needs to be built based on a `Notebooks` type of Docker image. This ensures it includes a Jupyter Notebook server, allowing interactive exploration of RAPIDS on a compute node with a GPU.

qa_pairs.question: How do you request an interactive session on a compute node with a single GPU, using an example command?
qa_pairs.answer: To request an interactive session on a compute node with a single GPU, you can use a command similar to this: `salloc --ntasks=1 --cpus-per-task=2 --mem=10G --gpus-per-node=1 --time=1:0:0 --account=def-someuser`.

qa_pairs.question: After a resource is granted, how do you start the RAPIDS shell on a GPU node within an interactive session?
qa_pairs.answer: Once the requested resource is granted, you start the RAPIDS shell on the GPU node by first loading the Apptainer module (`module load apptainer`) and then running `apptainer shell --nv rapids.sif`.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the `--nv` option when using `apptainer shell` to start the RAPIDS environment?
qa_pairs.answer: The `--nv` option binds the GPU driver on the host to the container, which allows the GPU device to be accessed from inside the Apptainer container.

qa_pairs.question: How can you verify that the GPU device is accessible within the Apptainer container once the shell prompt changes to `Apptainer>`?
qa_pairs.answer: After the shell prompt changes to `Apptainer>`, you can check the GPU stats in the container to confirm the GPU device is accessible by running `nvidia-smi`.

qa_pairs.question: How do you launch a Jupyter Notebook server in the RAPIDS environment from the Apptainer shell?
qa_pairs.answer: From the `Apptainer>` prompt, you can launch the Jupyter Notebook server in the RAPIDS environment using the command: `jupyter-lab --ip $(hostname -f) --no-browser`. The URL of the server will be displayed upon successful startup.

qa_pairs.question: What is a key feature of the RAPIDS v23.08 release regarding its conda environment within the container shell?
qa_pairs.answer: Starting with the RAPIDS v23.08 release, all packages are included in the base conda environment, which is activated by default in the container shell.

qa_pairs.question: What steps are necessary to connect to a Jupyter Notebook server if there is no direct Internet connection on the compute node?
qa_pairs.answer: If there is no direct Internet connection on a compute node, you would need to set up an SSH tunnel with port forwarding between your local computer and the GPU node to connect to the Jupyter Notebook.

qa_pairs.question: How do you submit a RAPIDS job to the Slurm scheduler?
qa_pairs.answer: Once your RAPIDS code is ready, you submit a RAPIDS job to the Slurm scheduler by writing a job submission script.

qa_pairs.question: What is considered a good practice when running jobs via a container on a compute node?
qa_pairs.answer: It is a good practice to use the local disk on a compute node when working via a container.

qa_pairs.question: In a Slurm submission script for a RAPIDS job, why and how would you copy files like the container image, code, and data to `$SLURM_TMPDIR`?
qa_pairs.answer: It is a good practice to copy your container image, RAPIDS code, and data to the local disk on a compute node via `$SLURM_TMPDIR` (e.g., `cp /path/to/rapids.sif ./`, `cp /path/to/your_rapids_code.py ./`, `cp -r /path/to/your_datasets ./`) to utilize faster temporary storage.

qa_pairs.question: How is the RAPIDS code executed within the Apptainer container in a Slurm submission script?
qa_pairs.answer: The RAPIDS code is executed using the command `apptainer exec --nv rapids.sif python ./my_rapids_code.py` within the submission script, ensuring GPU access for the Python script.

qa_pairs.question: How should results be handled and saved after a RAPIDS job completes within a Slurm submission script?
qa_pairs.answer: Any results generated within the `$SLURM_TMPDIR` directory should be copied back to your persistent project directory (e.g., `cp -r your_results ~/projects/def-someuser/username/`) before the job terminates.

qa_pairs.question: Where can users find comprehensive documentation for RAPIDS, including how to report issues?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can find comprehensive documentation for RAPIDS, along with information on how to stay connected and report issues, at the RAPIDS Docs website: [https://docs.rapids.ai/](https://docs.rapids.ai/).

qa_pairs.question: Where can users find example notebooks to quickly get started with RAPIDS?
qa_pairs.answer: A collection of example notebooks for quickly getting started with RAPIDS is available on GitHub at RAPIDS Notebooks: [https://github.com/rapidsai/notebooks](https://github.com/rapidsai/notebooks).

qa_pairs.question: Where can one find use cases and blogs related to RAPIDS applications?
qa_pairs.answer: Use cases and blogs for RAPIDS applications are available on RAPIDS on Medium: [https://medium.com/rapids-ai](https://medium.com/rapids-ai).
